message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Background & aim</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>The aim of this systematic review was to quantify the association between frailty and COVID-19 in relation to mortality in hospitalised patients.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Methods</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    Medline, Embase, Web of Science and the grey literature were searched for papers from inception to 10
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    September 2020; the search was re-run in Medline up until the 9
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    December 2020. Screening, data extraction and quality grading were undertaken by two reviewers. Results were summarised using descriptive statistics, including a meta-analysis of overall mortality; the relationships between frailty and COVID-19 mortality were summarised narratively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>2286 papers were screened resulting in 26 being included in the review. Most studies were from Europe, half from the UK, and one from Brazil; the median sample size was 242.5, median age 73.1 and 43.5% were female. 22/26 used the Clinical Frailty Scale; reported mortality ranged from 14 to 65%. Most, but not all studies showed an association between increasing frailty and a greater risk of dying. Two studies indicated a sub-additive relationship between frailty, COVID-19 and death, and two studies showed no association.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Whilst the majority of studies have shown a positive association between COVID-19 related death and increasing frailty, some studies suggested a more nuanced understanding of frailty and outcomes in COVID-19 is needed. Clinicians should exert caution in placing too much emphasis on the influence of frailty alone when discussing likely prognosis in older people with COVID-19 illness.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Key points</jats:title>
                  <jats:list list-type="bullet">
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Frailty is being used to assess the risk of dying from COVID-19</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Emerging studies demonstrate a complex relationship between frailty and COVID-19 related deaths</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Clinicians should exert caution in placing too much emphasis on the influence of frailty in older people with COVID-19</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                    <jats:list-item>
                      <jats:p>Researchers should ensure that frailty scales are used as designed when planning and reporting future research.</jats:p>
                    </jats:list-item>
                  </jats:list>
                </jats:sec>
title: What is the relationship between validated frailty scores and mortality for adults with COVID-19 in acute hospital care? A systematic review

reference.article-title: Frailty and COVID-19: A Systematic Scoping Review
